coredata.dc:description: Addressing energy and environmental issues, developing highly efficient, durable, and earth-abundant, electrocatalysts are crucial in oxygen evolution reaction (OER) for energy conversion and storage. Extensive research about the metal-organic framework (MOF) derived layered double hydroxides (LDH) and carbon-based electrocatalysts have been devoted, despite the exceptional performance in OER, yet the limited exposed active sites remain a challenge. Therefore, we reported a novel heteroatom-doped graphene quantum dots (GQDs) incorporated into MOF-derived NiFe-LDH. Taking advantage of abundant active and edge sites of GQDs and modifying the surface chemistry by chemical doping with foreign atoms, the designed heteroatom-doped GQDs/MOF-derived LDH exhibited superior catalytic performance low overpotential of 251 mV at a current density of 100 mA cm−2 in alkaline media. Our result confirmed the synergistic effect between the doped heteroatom and the uttermost exposure of heteroatom-doped GQDs/MOF-derived LDH's active sites on its surface, providing swift reactant's transportation and adequate contact for improving the OER performance.

head.ce:title: Unraveling the efficiency of heteroatom-doped graphene quantum dots incorporated MOF-derived bimetallic layered double hydroxide towards oxygen evolution reaction

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Addressing energy and environmental issues, developing highly efficient, durable, and earth-abundant, electrocatalysts are crucial in oxygen evolution reaction (OER) for energy conversion and storage. Extensive research about the metal-organic framework (MOF) derived layered double hydroxides (LDH) and carbon-based electrocatalysts have been devoted, despite the exceptional performance in OER, yet the limited exposed active sites remain a challenge. Therefore, we reported a novel heteroatom-doped graphene quantum dots (GQDs) incorporated into MOF-derived NiFe-LDH. Taking advantage of abundant active and edge sites of GQDs and modifying the surface chemistry by chemical doping with foreign atoms, the designed heteroatom-doped GQDs/MOF-derived LDH exhibited superior catalytic performance low overpotential of 251 mV at a current density of 100 mA cm−2 in alkaline media. Our result confirmed the synergistic effect between the doped heteroatom and the uttermost exposure of heteroatom-doped GQDs/MOF-derived LDH's active sites on its surface, providing swift reactant's transportation and adequate contact for improving the OER performance.

ce:section.ce:para: The diminution of fossil fuels has grown the environmental concerns and higher demand for global energy resources [1]. Hydrogen (H2) and oxygen (O2) generation through electrochemical water splitting has become promising than ever, converting storable chemical fuels from intermittent renewable energies [2]. Nevertheless, the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) of water splitting occurred at the cathode, facing sluggish kinetics and requiring greater overpotential due to the diverse-step electron transfer process proton-coupled, thus limiting the energy conversion efficiency. The state-of-art OER electrocatalysts, IrO2, and RuO2 take adequate overpotentials with appreciable turnover frequencies (TOFs) [3]. Hence, the widespread of its large-scale commercialization is hampered by the inferior stability, costive, and scarcity of these renewable energy technologies [4]. Intensive research efforts have been endeavored to explore other cost-effective, efficient, and durable OER electrocatalysts. Among those intensive researches, the new class of earth-rich elements, as well as phosphides [5,6], chalcogenides [7], and transition metal oxide [8] has been reported. Massive efforts have devoted in developing transition metal oxides of the first-row (Ni, Fe, Co, etc) for OER in alkaline media, owing to efficient catalytic activity [9,10].

Layered double hydroxides (LDHs), a two-dimensional (2D) unique lamellar structure featured with positive-charged brucite-like host interlayers, offered exceptional redox properties that are decent for occurred OER reaction [11,12]. Nickel-iron LDH (NiFe-LDH), a subtype of LDH, has been widely investigated owing to the highest catalytic performance toward OER, possessed a comparatively spacious structure, enabling reactants to diffuse rapidly and fasten the proton-coupled electron transfer in the OER process [13,14]. Simultaneously, developing metal-organic framework (MOFs) [15,16] and Prussian blue analogues (PBAs) [17,18], a complex-structured morphologies with large porosity and surface area precursor templates, that successfully enhanced the electron transport and innate capacity to coordinate of binding sites to sites of unsaturated upon the following modification to improve the modification design to derive metal-based materials had been thoroughly researched [19]. Developing a well-defined structure and composition-the changeable features of MOF-derived architecture allow it to obtain the desired properties, which opens up additional potential for future applications, such as MOF-derived LDH [20]. Particularly, their outstanding features of controllability, owing to their fine-tuned structure and composition derived the framework has proven to enhance the decent water oxidation activities delivery within LDH [21].

Given the limited conductivity and electrocatalytic active sites hinders its further application as an OER electrocatalyst, numerous strategies have been arisen to escalate the intrinsic activity of LDH, including exfoliation [22], element doping [23], and conductive substrates integration, such as graphene [24], and carbon nanotubes, [25]). The latter strategy of combining metal with carbon-based matrices has appealed to attention due to its massive surface area, robustness, eminent electrical conductivity, and proper surface functionality that grant access for assembling flexile covalent linkages at carbon atoms to facilitate the OER reaction [26]. The engineered electrical wires configurations or distances shortage between a solid surface and active sites played a crucial role in the electron transfer at the interface, tuning the overall reaction rates [27].

In particular, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a novel class of zero-dimensional nanocarbon, possessed nano-sized diameters (∼5–20 nm) of graphene fragments, held promising features for efficient OER electrocatalyst. GQDs have featured numerous edge and defect sites, which is beneficial for hosting the catalytic reactions, as it readily occurs in the edge than the basal plane [28]. Proposed experimental and theoretical innovative strategies have been achieved via engineered nanoarchitecture or molecular carbon, including surface functionalization, geometric structuring, and heteroatom doping [29]. Subsequent findings provided further proof of the critical role of doping-induced charge transfer for developing new electrocatalysts [30]. Introducing foreign heteroatoms with distinct electronegativity than carbon (B, N, P, S, Cl, I and Br) would shatter the GQD's electroneutrality, thus creating re-distributed charge areas that provide active sites for catalytic reactions [31]. Among heteroatoms doping, boron and nitrogen have been discovered to be efficient OER electrocatalysts [32]. Recently, Qian et al. reported an outstanding electrocatalytic activity from the construction of GQDs–Ni3S2 nanocomposites attributed to the electronic modulation between GQD and metal material [33]. Another study revealed that the designed N-doped GQDs/Co3O4 exhibited excellent catalytic activity that ascribed to the synergistic effect between metal and GQDs [34].

Recent research suggests that carbon with co-doped nitrogen and certain heteroatoms particularly boron, could considerably boost electrocatalytic performance due to the increased quantity of heteroatoms dopant and synergism between the doped heteroatoms [35]. In light of these findings, herein we developed novel catalytic materials by incorporation of heteroatom-doped GQDs at MOF-derived-NiFe-LDH that provided suitable spots and sufficient active sites for hosting OER reactions. Scheme 1 depicts a schematic illustration of the synthesis of heteroatom-doped GQDs/MOF-derived NiFe-LDH on Ni foam. The hydrothermal reaction strategy preserved the skeleton structures of MOFs, allowing for more efficient substrate diffusion while generating stable electrocatalysts. The designed heteroatom-doped GQDs/MOF-74-derived NiFe-LDH exhibited an ultralow overpotential of 251 mV at a current density of 100 mA/cm2 and possessed excellent durability over 24 h.

Nickel (II) nitrate hexahydrate (Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, ≥98%) was supplied from J. T. Baker. Iron (II) acetate, anhydrous, (Fe(C2H3O2)2, ≥98%) was purchased from Alfa Aesar. 2,5-dihydroxyterephthalic acid (H4DOT, ≥98%) was obtained from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. Nafion® 117 solution was supplied from Sigma-Aldrich (in water and less aliphatic alcohol mixture, ∼5%). D-(+)-Glucose, anhydrous, (C6H12O6, ≥99.5%), citric acid (C6H8O7, ACS reagent, ≥ 99.5%), ammonia solution (NH4OH, ≥ 25%) and boric acid (H3BO3, ACS reagent, ≥ 99.5%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Fisher Chemical provided methanol (MeOH, ≥99.9%), absolute ethanol (EtOH, ≥99.8%), N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF, ≥99.5%), and potassium hydroxide (KOH). Throughout this study, deionized water (DIW) (resistivity of 18.2 MΩ/cm at 25 °C) was utilized. All materials were utilized just as they were supplied, with no additional purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GQDs, B-GQDs, N-GQDs and B,N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), boron-doped GQDs (B-GQDs), nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs), and dual-doped boron and nitrogen (B,N-GQDs) were prepared via hydrothermal treatment. For synthesizing GQDs, citric acid aqueous solution (1 mg/mL, 40 mL) was moved in a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 3 h. Following cooling down to room temperature, the light yellow GQDs solution was dialyzed against DI water for 20 min using a dialysis tube (Spectrum Lab. Inc, 3000 Da) to detach impurities. Similarly, in a typical synthesis of B-GQDs, 4 g of glucose were diluted into 20 mL of DI water, followed with boric acid (16 mg, 0.4% wt) addition. The mixture was stirred at room temperature, then moved into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 3 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the resulting B-GQDs (light yellow solution) were dialyzed against DI water for 20 min using a dialysis tube to detach impurities and unreacted chemicals. For synthesizing N-GQDs, carbonization of citric acid and ammonia via hydrothermal treatment was carried out. Briefly, citric acid aqueous solution (1 mg/mL, 40 mL), followed by adding aqueous ammonia solution (5 mL, 25% wt), was stirred at room temperature and moved into Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 3 h. The resulting light yellow N-GQDs solution was also dialyzed against DI water for 20 min using a dialysis tube. The dual-doped B, N-GQDs were prepared using a similar process, with tiny modifications, apart from citric acid aqueous solution (1 mg/mL, 40 mL) as the carbon source, followed by adding aqueous ammonia solution (5 mL, 25% wt) and boric acid (4.8 mg, 12% wt).

Co-precipitation method was used to produce heteroatom-doped GQDs/NiFe-MOF-74 precursor, with a little modification [36]. In brief, 2,5-dihydroxyterephthalic acid (H4DOT) (0.45 mmol), Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (0.36 mmol), Fe(C2H3O2)2 (0.36 mmol), followed by adding GQDs, B-GQDs, N-GQDs and B,N-GQDs (0.5; 0.75; 1.0; 1.5 mL), were diluted then ultrasonicated for 30 min in a mixture of DMF, ethanol, and H2O (1:1:1 in ratio). The resultant solution was then put in a 100 mL autoclave with Teflon-lined for 24 h of heat treatment at 120 °C. The products were centrifugated to separate, washed multiple times with ethanol and methanol, followed by drying overnight at 60 °C in a vacuum environment.

Ni foam (FuCell Co., Ltd., Taoyuan, Taiwan) was utilized as the electrode for assessing OER performance. Ni foam (1cmx3cm) was cleaned by ultrasonication with 1.0 M HCl for 35 min before washing with DIW for 15 min. After cleaning, the Ni foam substrates were dried on a hotplate at 70 °C. The active electrode region of 1cmx1cm was defined using epoxy tapes (3 M Company, thickness 60 μm). 10 mg of electrocatalysts were dispersed in of isopropyl alcohol (IPA, 750 μL), DIW (200 μL), and Nafion® 117 solution (50 μL) to make the electrocatalysts ink. To further homogenize the catalytic ink, ultrasonication was conducted. The catalysts ink was then drop coated over Ni foam (loading: 3.0 mg/mL). After that, the post-treatment electrodes were put on a hotplate (70 °C) to dry.

All electrochemical experiments were performed in an atypical three-electrode setup of O2-saturated 1.0 M KOH (at room temperature) controlled by Autolab potentiostat/galvanostat (PGSTAT302 N with FRA32 M, Metrohm Autolab). The working electrode was Ni foam substrates coated with the as-prepared electroactive materials, whereas the counter and reference electrodes were platinum rod and Hg/HgO (in 1 M NaOH), respectively. 10 mg of catalysts were mixed in 1 mL of mixed solution to make the catalysts suspension. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was conducted with a scan rate of 5 mV s−1 and corrected with iR compensation. After each step, the ohmic drop was measured by impedance spectroscopy and was employed for iR compensation. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was taken in the frequency region of 100 kHz - 0.1 Hz, and using 5 mV amplitude. The electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) was measured using cyclic voltammograms data of the non-faradaic zone (1.11–1.21 V vs. RHE) at different scan rates (20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 mV s−1). Using Equation (1), all potentials in this work were transformed into potential vs reversible hydrogen electrode:where EHg/HgO/1 M NaOH is the converted potential vs. Hg/HgO, E oHg/HgO/1 M NaOH=0.165 V at 25 °C, and the experimentally measured potential vs. the RHE reference is denoted as ERHE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of GQDs and heteroatom-doped GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The heteroatom-doped GQDs, were synthesized through a facile one-step hydrothermal method of the carbon source (glucose and citric acid), boric acid as boron source and ammonia as the nitrogen source. The technical details of the various reaction condition are summarized in Table S1 (See Supporting Information). The schematic illustration of the as-obtained GQDs solution in the presence of the different dopant molecules was presented in Fig. S1. Afterward, Raman spectroscopy was conducted to investigate the structure of pristine GQDs and heteroatom-doped GQDs. As shown in Fig. S2, pristine GQDs displayed prominent peaks of typical graphene attributes at ∼1332 cm−1 and ∼1596 cm−1 assigned to the D and G bands, respectively. The D-mode was a disorder-induced band, corresponded by a structural disorder of defects and edges of sp3 carbons, while G-mode emerged by deformation of C–C in the sp2 carbon [37]. Particularly, prominent red-shift was observed in the D band after introducing the dopants in GQDs, that associated with the distortion of graphene lattice upon bond reconstruction (the shortening of N–C or B–C bonds instead of C–C bonds) and defect pinning. Meanwhile, the blue shift of the G band to lower frequencies suggested a fluctuation in carrier density due to hole doping that promotes lattice ion interaction as a result of dopant introduction [38]. Furthermore, these vibrations were driven by the displacements of carbon atoms that generate defects. The observed ID/IG (Fig. S2) was evaluated to measure the degree of disorder, affording a value of 0.54 for pristine GQDs, suggesting that defects were present [39]. Approximately, the sharp increase of ID/IG ratio were observed after the single-doping and got doubledafter dual-doping of B,N-GQDs, giving the value of 1.10. These were attributed to the greater defect quantity caused by the intercalation of B and N dopants, as well as the creation of vacancies over single dopant only, thus, beneficial for reconstructing metal-carbon structures to enhance its catalytic activity [40].

The transmission electron spectroscopy (TEM) were employed to observe the morphology of the as-obtained heteroatom-doped GQDs. Fig. 1 a showed the images of B,N-GQDs, depicting the fairly uniform particle distribution between 1 and 6 nm, with an average size of 3.3 ± 0.7 nm (Fig. 1b). The high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image (inset of Fig. 1a) revealed a clear lattice fringe with an interplanar spacing of 0.214 nm indicated the strong crystallinity, which matched the hexagonal lattice spacing along the (100) direction present in the graphene structure [37]. Similarly, strong crystallinity were also spotted in the pristine GQDs, B-GQDs, and N-GQDs (Figs. S3a–c) with larger particle size (±5–6 nm), which confirm the interlayer spacing of graphene structure. GQDs had distinct photoluminescence (PL) activity that displayed intense fluorescence due to the strong quantum effect and edge effect. Photoluminescence excitation-emission-intensity spectra of dual-doped B,N-GQDs (Fig. 1c) were observed by collecting the emission spectra of the sample with the excitation at various excitation wavelengths. As shown in Fig. 1c, the excitation wavelength at 380 nm and 300 nm gave a strong emission centered at 440 nm. Interestingly, these dual shiftings were ascribed to the intercalation of the dual dopants within GQDs, due to the high electron-withdrawing of N and B atoms within the conjugated C [41]. Particularly, the emission intensity of B,N-GQDs is higher than pristine GQDs and single-doped GQDs (B-GQDs and N-GQDs) (Figs. S4a–c), that contributed by surface effects of highly functional O-groups upon the incorporation of the dopants (B or N), indicating the highest functional O-groups created due to the strong affinity of B and N atoms to the GQDs [42].

The chemical state and composition of the B,N-GQDs were investigated using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS survey scans (Fig. S5) of GQDs and heteroatom-doped GQDs depicted the existence of C, O, B (in the B-GQDs and B,N-GQDs) and N (in the N-GQDs and B,N-GQDs). The C 1s XPS spectra of the B,N-GQDs (Fig. 1d) suggested the existence of CC (284.3 eV), C–N (285.7 eV), CO (286.7 eV), and O–CO (288.2 eV) carbon-containing functional groups, similar in the N-GQDs, while pristine GQDs and B-GQDs acquiring C–O presence (at 288.3 and 286.3 eV) (Fig. S6). In addition, the high resolution of B 1s (Fig. 1e) core level spectra for B,N-GQDs revealed three prominent peaks ascribed to B–C (187.1 eV), B–N (191.6 eV), and B–O (194.8 eV). Additionally, the N 1s spectra (Fig. 1f) suggest the presence of and pyridinic (399.3 eV), pyrrolic (400.8 eV), and graphitic N (401.5 eV), respectively, confirming the successful dual-doping of B and N within the GQDs framework. The elemental composition of the heteroatom-doped GQDs was summarized in Table S2 (Supporting Information), suggesting the successive doping process.

The heteroatom-doped GQDs/NiFe-MOF-74 were prepared through a facile hydrothermal process, illustrated in Fig. 2 a. The hydrothermal behavior of the as-synthesized electrocatalysts under various heteroatom-doped GQDs was studied. Fig. 2b shows the compiled XRD pattern of the as-synthesized heteroatom-doped GQDs/NiFe-MOF-74. All diffraction peaks of heteroatom-doped GQDs/NiFe-MOF-74 showed two distinct diffraction peaks, indexed to the (110) and (300) planes originating from the structure of NiFe-MOF-74 [43]. To asses an advance detail about NiFe-MOF-74 with heteroatom doped GQDs incorporation, Raman spectroscopy was evaluated, as shown in Fig. 2c. Raman spectra of NiFe-MOF-74 were dominated by MOF's skeleton of the aromatic rings, phenolate, and carboxylate, such as (COO−), (C–O), and (CC)aromatic ring. The peak at ∼1609 and ∼1290 cm−1 were assigned to stretching vibrations of benzene ring within organic linker [44] and the after-deprotonated C–O species of the carboxylic acid groups [45], respectively, while peak at ∼1421 cm−1 was assigned to (Ni–Oligand) bond vibration and C–H bonds of a benzene ring [46]. The coordinatedcarboxylate and phenolate groups with metal ions formed metal−oxygen bonds, supporting MOF structure. These MOF's vibrational modes were sensitive to any structural modification [47]. Interestingly, all the pristine and heteroatom-doped GQDs/NiFe-MOPF-74 yielded highly characteristic of extended MOF-74 structures, affirming the XRD result. As shown in Fig. 2c, the broadened B,N-GQDs peak at ∼1350 cm−1 and ∼1570 cm−1 were invested in all the pristine and heteroatom-doped GQDs/NiFe-MOPF-74, confirming its successful incorporation by creating defect-rich network. The fact that these peaks appeared highest as compared to pristine GQDs and single-doped GQDs (B-GQDs and N-GQDs), confirming the dual-doped effect suggested the introduction of two dopants of B and N significantly created more defect and edge sites within MOF-74's framework [39].

SEM images revealed the morphology of the as-synthesized NiFe-MOF-74 with various types of GQDs, the apparent rhombic structure was observed after introducing GQDs (Fig. S8b), B-GQDs (Fig. S8c), N-GQDs (Fig. S8d), and B,N-GQDs (Fig. 2d), that obviously similar to that of NiFe-MOF-74 (Fig. S8a). The possible in-situ growth of heteroatom-doped GQDs/MOF-74 is proposed as follows. The O-rich functional groups on the surface of GQDs (-OH and –COOH) provided proper conditions for metal clusters (Ni and Fe) to bind on, followed by nucleation and crystal growth after the ligand addition [48]. This in-situ growth mechanism allowed particles (in a particular size) to trap during the growth process. NiFe-MOF-74 had an average pore diameter of 3–8 nm [43], in which easier for heteroatom-doped GQDs (3.3 ± 0.7 nm) to be trapped in.

According to our previous study [36], the transition of NiFe-MOF-74 into NiFe-LDH was easily accomplished by immersing the NiFe-MOF-74 in alkaline solution at room temperature (∼25 °C). To go extensively into the transformation during electrochemical analysis, the XRD was performed for heteroatom-doped GQDs/NiFe-MOF-74 electrocatalysts after immersion in electrolyte for 30 min, as shown in Fig. S9. Fig. S9 depicted the compiled XRD pattern after 30 min immersion in electrolyte, provided the strong evident feature for spontaneously deriving heteroatom-doped GQDs/NiFe-LDH of significant peak shifting of XRD pattern in regards of the crystalline phase. All the samples exhibited a similar pattern corresponding to the α-NiFe(OH)2, which attributed to NiFe-LDH, as indexed to JCPDS No. 38–0715 [49], confirming the spontaneously deriving of heteroatom-doped GQDs/NiFe-MOF-74 in alkaline media. The size, shape, and structure of the as-obtained samples were evaluated using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). SEM images of heteroatom-doped GQDs/MOF-derived NiFe-LDH (Figs. S10a–c) revealed a nanosheet-like surface structure that exhibits similarities with LDH structure, were acquired for all of the samples which marked the successful transformation step to NiFe-LDH. The TEM images (Fig. S11a) demonstrated the irregular and thin nanosheet-like structure of the as-obtained dual-doped B,N-GQDs/MOF-derived LDH with the particle size around ∼70 nm. The elemental mapping investigations of X-ray energy dispersive spectrometry (EDS) reveal the uniform hybridization of Ni and Fe over the structure (Figs. S11b–c).

To further gain insight from the B,N-GQDs/NiFe-MOF-74 upon transformation process, XPS was performed to study the chemical structure and composition before and after the transformation process. The XPS full-survey spectra of B,N-GQDs/NiFe-MOF-74 (Fig. S12) corroborated the existence of Ni, Fe, C, N, O, and B, indicating the heteroatom-doped GQDs were successfully integrated within NiFe-MOF-74. This result was also acquired by B,N-GQDs/MOF-derived-NiFe-LDH (Fig. 3 a), suggesting that no major compositional change was observed after the transformation process. The Ni 2p core-level spectra of B,N-GQDs/NiFe-MOF-74 (Fig. 3b) displayed two doublets spin-orbit of Ni 2p1/2, and Ni 2p3/2 which located at 873.7 and 856.5 eV for Ni2+, and 876.5 and 858.8 eV for Ni3+, respectively. Shakeup satellites (denoted as ‘Sat’) were observed at 882.1 and 863.8 eV. The Fe 2p core-level spectra showed significant Fe2+ peaks at 713.0 (2p3/2) and 725.0 eV (2p1/2). As a consequence, the oxidation state of Fe has been verified to be 2+, in good agreement with the previous work [43]. In contrast with the previous study that confirmed the Ni oxidation state of pristine NiFe-MOF-74 was 2+, the Ni oxidation state of B,N-GQDs/NiFe-MOF-74 was confirmed to be 2+ and 3+. Remarkably, the newly emerged peak assigned to Ni3+ could indicate the conjugated carbon-metal network within NiFe-MOF-74 that led the formation of high valence Ni3+, as the result of the arising of numerous defects by the oxygen-rich functional groups and upon the B, N doping [39]. Following the transformation process, similar chemical alterations were noticed. Ni2+ at 856.5 eV (2p3/2) of B,N-GQDs/NiFe-MOF-74 moved into lower energy 856.3 eV (ΔE ∼0.2 eV), whereas Ni3+ at 858.8 eV (2p3/2) shifted into 857.5 eV (ΔE ∼1.3 eV). The negative transition raised the density of the electron within Ni sites, along with the broader Ni2+ peak, decreasing the valence state through the phase of nickel-hydroxide formation at the cost of the coordination of Ni–Fe local environment as the active site [50]. Along with its lesser coordination number and stronger H2O energy absorption, Ni2+ triggered OOH species to deprotonate, generating oxygen and contributes to enhanced OER performance [51].

An identical trend was surprisingly identified in the core-level spectra of Fe 2p (Fig. 3b). The Fe2+ XPS peak was negatively displaced to 711.2 eV (ΔE ∼0.7 eV) and emerged a new peak at 714.1 eV, suggesting a generation of high-valent Fe3+, which was followed by satellite peaks at 718.3 and 726.0 eV [44]. The oxidation status of Fe in B,N-GQDs/MOF-derived-NiFe-LDH is predominantly considered to be 3+. The development of a thin amorphous layer on the surface of the LDH causes a considerable weakening of identifiable Ni3+ or Fe2+ signals and the appearance of strong Ni2+ and Fe3+ signals following the transformation process. Thus, the heteroatom-doping played major role for carbon and MOF-derived NiFe-LDH coupling, causing electronic environment distortions and Ni/Fe–O–C, Ni–C/Fe–C or Ni–O–C/Fe–O–C or Ni/Fe–O–C linkages bond formation, suggesting more defects are created that serve as Ni2+ and Fe3+ cations’ nucleation and adsorption sites, thereby kinetically boosting the OER [52]. In order to trace the OER enhancement activity, the Ni:Fe atomic ratio by XPS before and after the transformation process was probed (Table S3). As demonstrated in Table S3, the Ni:Fe atomic ratios of B,N-GQDs/NiFe-MOF-74 and B,N-GQDs/MOF-derived-NiFe-LDH were comparable (∼1:1), with only a slight decrease. Particularly, the small Ni:Fe atomic ratio declination after transformation may be attributed to changed local structure during alkaline solution immersion, which made high-valent metal sites stable at low overpotential under alkaline preparation conditions. Table S4 shows the atomic composition of elements estimated from XPS pre and post transformation process. The atomic composition had almost the same quantitative value after transformation, validating the homogeneity of the metal composition from the MOF as precursor, that served as a template for the formation of transition-metal hydroxides, resulting in adjustable metal composition and ratios.

To begin with, we performed the electrocatalytic performance of heteroatom-doped GQDs MOF-derived-NiFe-LDH with various loading amounts for OER, as shown in Fig. S13. Noticeably, the loading amount of 1 mL of heteroatom-doped GQDs into MOF-derived-NiFe-LDH exhibited the highest electrocatalytic performance, implying optimized loading concentration of GQDs within MOF-derived-NiFe-LDH, compared to the other loading amounts. The excess amount of heteroatom-doped GQDs might cut the electrical conductivity of MOF-derived-NiFe-LDH, thus contributing to the degraded OER activity [53]. Giving a better understand of the supportive role of heteroatom-doped GQDs in MOF-derived-NiFe-LDH, the electrocatalytic performance was performed, as shown in Fig. 4 a. In comparison to other electrodes, the Ni foam (bare) had the weakest electrocatalytic performance, affording 382 mV at 10 mA cm−2 of overpotential. Specifically, the B,N-GQDs/MOF-derived-NiFe-LDH (abbreviated as B,N-GQDs/MOF-d-LDH) demonstrated the best electrocatalytic activity with a lower overpotential (η100) of 251 mV at 100 mA cm−2 as compared to the pristine GQDs and single-doped GQDs incorporated in MOF-derived-NiFe-LDH. Particularly, the OER performance was escalated by the introduction of pristine GQDs in the first place, due to the defect-rich environment that provide better electron transportation by Raman study. Clearly, the OER performance increased upon the introduction of heteroatom-doping. The OER performance is in the order of GQDs/MOF-derived-NiFe-LDH (η100 = 287 mV) < B-GQDs/MOF-derived-NiFe-LDH (η100 = 282 mV) < N-GQDs/MOF-derived-NiFe-LDH (η100 = 261 mV) < B,N-GQDs/MOF-derived-NiFe-LDH (η100 = 251 mV). In consequence, the MOF-derived-NiFe-LDH incorporated with dual-doped of B and N GQDs exhibited higher OER activity than that with single-doped one, confirming the synergistic effect from two different types of dopants featured with distinct electronegativity.

The catalytic mechanism in those dual-doped B,N-GQDs embedded in MOF-derived NiFe LDH begin with the adjacent C atom next to N is polarized, while B atoms allow the adsorption of H/OH, continued by chemical bonding with Ni/Fe active sites. Notably, the different types of dopants introduced in single-doped GQDs/MOF-derived LDH demonstrated distinct electrocatalytic performance. N-doped GQDs/MOF-derived LDH showed more efficient electrocatalytic activity toward OER than the B-doped GQDs/MOF-derived LDH. As reported before, N's presence in the graphene material promotes the graphene ability to transfer an electron to the nearest carbon atoms, which is beneficial for such OER reactions [54]. Whereas B-doping induced graphene matrix to be polarized that favorable to electrocatalysis reaction, unfortunately, the graphene matrix distortion caused by B-doping at the vacant C sites made the out-of-plane of the matrix remain in the same position due to the instability that practically hinders the defect creation from giving access to OER [55]. In fact, the dual-doped GQDs/MOF-derived LDH showed significant improvement to the OER performance, thanks to its synergistic effect of dual-dopants (B and N). N atoms (with lower atomic radius than C) that doped into the graphene lattice could promote B doping by counteracting the N-induced lattice pressure stress (vice versa) [56], proving major role of dual-doped GQDs to the OER enhancement.

The inherent electrochemical activity of MOF-derived LDH with different heteroatom-doped GQDs was further explored using the Tafel slope, which is directly connected to the electrochemical reaction kinetics for OER, as shown in Fig. 4b. As presented in Fig. 4b, the B,N-GQDs/MOF-derived-NiFe-LDH had the lowest Tafel slope of 34.9 mV dec−1 in contrast to GQDs/MOF-derived-NiFe-LDH (47.4 mV dec−1), B-GQDs/MOF-derived-NiFe-LDH (43.7 mV dec−1), and N-GQDs/MOF-derived-NiFe-LDH (39.8 mV dec−1), marked as the most favorable electrocatalytic kinetics of heteroatom-doped GQDs/MOF-derived-NiFe-LDH. The elevated OER electroactivity of associated electrocatalysts associated with the disclosure of the active sites was confirmed by calculating the Cdl, which is utilized to estimate the electrocatalysts' relative electrochemical surface area (ECSA). The linear slope fitting is utilized to express the electrode's Cdl, that is directly equivalent to the ECSA. As presented in Fig. 4c, the B,N-GQDs/MOF-derived-NiFe-LDH exhibited higher Cdl of 10.8 mF cm−2, compared to that with GQDs (5.94 mF cm−2), B-doped GQDs (7.84 mF cm−2), and N-doped GQDs (10.2 mF cm−2). This verified that the heteroatom-doped depicting major role in creating defective sites, and further escalated by the synergistic effect of dual-doping, for providing spots of transition metal cations of LDH binding that drastically tune the OER, in well agreement with the result from the Raman spectra (Fig. S2). Nevertheless, the heteroatom-doped GQDs/MOF-derived-NiFe-LDH give a better access to fully-exposed active sites and current collection equilibrium, and alter the MOF-derived-NiFe-LDH electronic nature, and even, on the whole, enhance the charge transfer path kinetics [41]. The electrochemical evaluation of the as-obtained MOF-derived LDH revealed that the hybrid structure of heteroatom-doped GQDs/MOF-derived-NiFe-LDH enabled modifications via the MOF-derived-NiFe-LDH layer, owing to facilitated limitless active sites exposure and linkages initiation of heteroatom-dopants to the C atom that fastens the charge transfer kinetics, thus effectively escalate the OER performance [57].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Role of single-doped and dual-doped GQDs/MOF-derived-LDH to its electrocatalytic performance
ce:section.ce:para: In order to assess an advance detail about single and dual-doped GQDs in contributing to the OER performance, the ketonic group ratio was calculated from XPS. The drastically enhanced OER activities were found contributed by the increase of ketonic (CO) groups, owing to abundant active carbon by less-stable oxygen groups removal and escalated conductivity and C–O groups, which later found that ketonic (CO) groups plays major role toward OER than C–O groups [58]. The increasing ratio intensities of ketonic groups (Fig. 5 a) in heteroatom-doped GQDs, notably, the dual-doped B,N-GQDs that exceed ∼19.8 suggesting the increasing number of ketonic groups upon heteroatom-dopants introduction, which significantly contributes to the OER performance. The doping effect was further studied by soft X-Ray absorption spectroscopy. The rising-edge position in the normalized C K-edge spectra B,N-GQDs/NiFe-MOF-74 exhibited a clear difference from GQDs/NiFe-MOF-74 (Fig. 5b). The intense feature near ∼284.0 and ∼290.0 eV corresponded to the C 1s transition to sp2-unoccupied π* (transitions of the benzene ring) and σ* (transitions for C–C single bonds) of graphitic states, respectively, that confirms the preserved graphitic framework inside GQDs/NiFe-MOF-74. These features were apparently stronger and shifted to higher energy (approximately 0.1–0.3 eV) in the B,N-GQDs/NiFe-MOF-74, revealing an indicative energy increase in the final state of 3d after dual-doped with B and N [59]. Another characteristic peak arising near 284.7 eV was contributed by carbon excitation in the sp2 framework into the π* band, where single-bonded carbon-oxygen (C–O) encountered configurations, either in hydroxyl or epoxy [60]. The less intense peak was observed in dual-doped B,N-GQDs/NiFe-MOF-74, indicating no strong carbon-oxygen (C–O) configurations, that hold C framework preservation after introducing dopants. A stronger resonance peak at 286.8 eV contributed by local geometry changes upon rippled and folded within GQDs as dual-doped (B and N) were introduced, suggesting an intense interaction between, due to the deformation of GQDs framework by the B and N synergistic coupling effect, creating numerous defects and linkages to C atom's network, hence, allowing Ni and Fe ions adsorbed easier on the graphene hexagons [61]. Furthermore, spectra levels at 288.0 and 289.7 eV contain additional energy features that are indicative of additional functional groups of carbon–oxygen, most probably the Ni, Fe and graphene interface oxygen bridge, suggesting interruption of oxygen functionalization to the local sp2 bonding configuration that create additional edges and defects [62]. These changes are associated with strong coupling between Ni and Fe ions and graphene framework via Ni–C/Fe–C or Ni–O–C/Fe–O–C (including nickel carbonate Ni–O–CO) covalent bonding structures [56]. This intense intensity were observed in dual-doped B,N-GQDs/NiFe-MOF-74, reflecting higher edges and defects created and stronger coupling of Ni and Fe ions with graphene framework, due to the successive synergistic coupling effects made by dual doped B and N into graphene network.

ce:section.ce:section-title: B,N-GQDs/MOF-derived-NiFe-LDH comparison with pristine NiFe LDH and IrO2
ce:section.ce:para: Identical tests were done for various commercial catalysts, including pristine-NiFe-LDH and IrO2, to compare the OER performance. To further clarify the heteroatom-doping impact of GQDs, the MOF-derived-NiFe-LDH, acquired from the prior finding, was also included in the measurements [36].

As shown in Fig. 6 a, the B,N-GQDs/MOF-derived-NiFe-LDH exhibited excellent OER performance with the lowest overpotential of 251 mV, followed by IrO2 (293 mV), MOF-derived-NiFe-LDH (341 mV) and pristine-NiFe-LDH (405 mV) at a current density of 100 mA cm−2. Apparently, the integration of heteroatom-dopants into GQDs incorporated in MOF-derived-NiFe-LDH enhanced OER performance significantly. Revealed from XPS analysis, the synergistic coupling effect of B and N atoms created numerous defects and linkages to C atom's network, hence, allowing Ni and Fe ions adsorbed easier on the graphene hexagons [61]. Upon the introduction of B and N atoms, the dual-doped mechanism of B and N was demonstrated, with B atoms acting as electron acceptors, while N atoms as electron donors, both atoms could reduce the valence band while lifting the conduction band, resulted in de-localization of electron and graphene activation [63]. The kinetic study was further suggested that the B,N-GQDs/MOF-derived-NiFe-LDH had given the lowest Tafel slope of 34.9 mV dec−1, in contrast to IrO2 (61.3 mV dec−1), MOF-derived-NiFe-LDH (48.9 mV dec−1), and pristine NiFe-LDH (96.2 mV dec−1) as shown in Fig. 6b, indicating the advanced kinetic properties obtained from the synergistic effect of dual-doping. Additionally, chronopotentiometry was performed in 1.0 M KOH solution measured at 100 mA cm−2 to assess operational durability (Fig. 6c). Correspondingly, B,N-GQDs/MOF-derived-NiFe-LDH demonstrated good stability over 24 h, with a little increased potential trend (3.4% loss) as compared to commercial IrO2 with a relatively increased potential trend (7.3% loss). The outstanding stability of B,N-GQDs/MOF-derived-NiFe-LDH was further showed by OER polarization curves (Fig. 6d), with no significant change seen during a long-term durability test of 24 h. Identically, the retained morphology after a long-term durability was shown (Fig. S15). Furthermore, the B,N-GQDs/MOF-derived-NiFe-LDH was capable to maintain its elemental compositions after stability as shown by EDS, with Ni:Fe ratio of ∼1 (Table S5). Hence, B,N-GQDs/MOF-derived-NiFe-LDH maintained excellent electrocatalytic stability during long-term testing durability in alkaline solution.

To summarize, we effectively designed heteroatom-doped NiFe-LDH via spontaneously deriving heteroatom-doped NiFe-MOF-74 for an efficient OER in alkaline conditions. The designated porous nature of MOF-74 and the synergistic effect between dopants, the hybrid structure of heteroatom-doped GQDs/MOF-derived-NiFe-LDH provided better access to fully-exposed active sites for the OER, and the newly formed Ni–C/Fe–C or Ni–O–C/Fe–O–C or Ni/Fe–O–C linkages acted as a prominent hotspot for transfer of electrons. Taking advantage of the controlled metal ratio upon transformation process, the dual-doped B,N-GQDs/MOF-derived-NiFe-LDH demonstrated outstanding activity, affording an ultralow overpotential of 251 mV to reach 100 mA cm−2 of a current density, a corresponding Tafel slope of 34.9 mV dec−1, and displayed a high stability over 24 h. Based on the current findings in this subsequent work, we believe that this novel strategy will offer new enlightenment in designing a hybrid structure of MOF-derivative electrocatalysts, allowing researchers to expand the scope of MOFs and its possible structure derivatives, and further investigate super-effective electrocatalyst.
